g.data-narration: Welcome to Loading Constants in ARM Assembly. This essential skill enables efficient constant loading in embedded systems.
ARM instructions have limited encoding space. Only small constants fit directly in instructions, requiring special techniques for larger values.
g.text: ARM uses 32-bit instruction encoding with limited space for immediate values.
Only 12 bits available for constants - requiring clever techniques.

g.data-narration: ARM uses rotated 8-bit encoding: any 8-bit value (0-255) rotated right by 0-15 positions creates valid immediates.
The MOV instruction loads small constants (0-255) directly into registers. It's the fastest method for small values.
g.text: Syntax: MOVS Rd, #imm (S suffix updates flags)

g.data-narration: MOV can use shift operations to create larger constants from small immediates by shifting left.
MVN loads the bitwise NOT of a constant. Useful for -1 and other bit patterns that are complements of valid immediates.
For constants too large for immediate encoding, LDR with literal pool loads from memory using PC-relative addressing.
g.text: LDR with Literal Pool - Large Constants
Syntax: LDR Rd, =const (pseudo-instruction, assembler decides implementation)

rect.text: LDR R0, =0x12345678 ; Load large constant via literal pool

g.data-narration: The assembler automatically chooses: MOV/MVN if constant fits, or LDR with PC-relative load from literal pool.
ADR loads PC-relative addresses into registers, useful for accessing local data. Generates ADD or SUB instructions.
g.text: Syntax: ADR Rd, label (loads address, NOT data)

rect.text: ADR R0, my_data ; Load address of my_data into R0

g.text: Use LDR for data at address

g.data-narration: MOVW loads lower 16 bits, MOVT loads upper 16 bits. Together they can load any 32-bit value using two instructions.
g.text: Thumb-2 instructions for loading any 32-bit value in two steps

g.data-narration: Example: Initialize R0-R3 with values 10, 20, 30, 40. All fit in immediate field, so MOV works perfectly.

rect.text: MOVS R0, #10 MOVS R1, #20 MOVS R2, #30 MOVS R3, #40

g.data-narration: Example: Load 0x12345678. This exceeds immediate range, so LDR with literal pool is required.

rect.text: LDR R0, =0x12345678 ; Assembler generates LDR from literal pool

g.data-narration: Example: Load -1 (0xFFFFFFFF). Use MVN with 0, or LDR with literal pool for simplicity.

rect.text: LDR R0, =-1 ; Simpler: use literal pool

g.data-narration: Summary: Choose MOV for 0-255, MVN for complements, LDR for large values, MOVW+MOVT for any 32-bit.
Exam tips: Know immediate limitations, choose appropriate method, remember MOVW/MOVT combo, use LDR for flexibility.
g.text: • LDR =const is most versatile - use it!
• ADR loads addresses, LDR loads data
